text: The surgeon inserts the first port in the left subcostal region and performs scope dissection using the robotic camera to create the initial space.

The surgeon inserts the first port under visual guidance and uses a bipolar cutter to create additional space for the placement of two more robotic ports. The posterior rectus sheath is visible on the floor, while the rectus abdominis is seen on the roof of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the medial edge at the junction of the anterior rectus sheath (ARS) and posterior rectus sheath (PRS), with the linea seminalis laterally. The retromuscular space is visible in the center. The surgeon uses spinal G needles to determine the direction and depth for placing the robotic port, taking care to avoid injuring the left-sided inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon clears the attachment of the posterior rectus sheath to the anterior rectus sheath, working within a confined space of approximately seven centimeters. The camera and instruments are positioned in this limited area to facilitate dissection and create space for entering the correct anatomical plane.

The video displays the junction of the posterior rectus sheath and the anterior rectus sheath. The surgeon prepares to divide the posterior rectus sheath approximately three millimeters below this junction.

The surgeon uses a scissor with a cotter current to take backward strokes, dividing the bridge of tissue to widen the space. The robotic platform is shown to be sensitive to space, requiring a horizontal field for effective operation.

The surgeon continues to clear the left retromuscular space and divides the posterior rectus sheath, taking care not to breach the peritoneum. Dissection progresses on the caudal side, with the left side being caudal and the right side cranial. The robotic ports are positioned laterally on the left side.

The cut edge of the posterior rectus sheath is visible, with the peritoneum abutting the thinned-out linea alba. A small layer of the linea alba is preserved on the peritoneum to prevent complications due to its thinness.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully peeling off the peritoneum from the infraumbilical divarication, marking the end of the divarication area.

The surgeon shifts focus cranially, using backward strokes with scissors to divide the posterior rectus sheath (PRS) and any intervening tissue between the main incision and a new incision. The procedure is performed in tight spaces, utilizing the robotic platform to enhance precision and comfort.

The video shows the thinned-out peritoneum as a translucent membrane on the floor, separating the abdominal contents from the surgical field. Above, the divarication of the recti is visible, representing the thinned connection between the two rectus abdominis muscles, which will be repaired. The surgical movements appear sped up due to video playback speed but are executed with precision to avoid creating false spaces.

The central area of the screen displays the patient's hernia. The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the hernia site, avoiding direct manipulation of the hernia itself due to the thinness of the peritoneum and hernia sac. The dissection includes caudal movements, visible on the screen, to prevent potential tears or buttonholes.

The hernia appears prominently, resembling a volcano, as the surgeon performs circumferential dissection around it. The focus shifts to the posterior rectus sheath on the right side, identified by the orientation of its fibers. The surgeon develops the incision cranially and caudally to match the left side, using a two-dimensional view on the screen.

The surgeon is dissecting the loose areolar tissue within the right-sided retro muscular space, navigating between abdominal layers akin to a club sandwich. The dissection progresses cranially, aiming to reach a zone where the inter-rectus distance is approximately two to three centimeters.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the cranial end, focusing on the left-sided posterior rectus sheath. A large preperitoneal space has been created on the right side, facilitating the robotic approach. The surgical field opens up, revealing clear anatomical landmarks for precise incision and entry.

The surgeon completes the creation of a large space by connecting the right and left retro muscular spaces with the midline preperitoneal space. These distinct anatomical spaces unite to form a common large space, visible in the surgical field, which will accommodate the placement of prostheses to reinforce the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon focuses on the remaining tissue of the hernia hanging from the roof, carefully dissecting to preserve as much peritoneum as possible. The peritoneum is visibly thinned out, and the surgeon begins cutting and dividing it from the skin and subcutaneous tissue to ensure a tension-free closure of the defect.

The surgeon peels the peritoneum from the skin and subcutaneous tissue on the right side, completing the dissection. The posterior elements are positioned on the floor with minimal tissue loss, marking a significant milestone in the procedure.

The surgeon clears fibro fatty tissue from the infraumbilical area, revealing the right-sided inferior epigastric vessels. Further lateral dissection is performed, carefully avoiding the neurovascular bundles visible from the floor to the roof, ensuring their preservation with robotic precision.

The surgeon completes the dissection by removing a small remaining tissue segment. The umbilical hernia is visible, and the peritoneum has been stripped from the divarication. The surgeon begins planning the closure of the herniated defect and plication of the divarication using a barbed suture. Needle drivers with scissors are used for the suture cut. The surgeon demonstrates a camera inversion technique, making the anteriorly placed divarication appear as if it is on the floor of the surgical field on the screen.

The surgeon takes a small bite from the subcutaneous tissue to decrease seroma formation. Conventional suturing is performed for the supraumbilical component, using backhand suturing techniques. Near the port, a horizontal mattress suture is applied for better strength and apposition. Suturing continues towards the umbilical defect with a small overlap to ensure a tight repair.

The surgeon closes the defect in the posterior elements, including the peritoneum, using a tailored mesh. A scale is used to measure the dissected cavity, and the mesh is trimmed and placed flat within the cavity. The cavity is deflated and re-inflated to ensure proper mesh placement, marking the end of the surgery.
